title: Long-tail cast on
subtitle: The standard knitting cast-on: stretchy, decorative on the edge, fast to work once it clicks.
excerpt: A long-tail cast-on uses two yarns from a single ball: a measured tail and the working yarn. The right needle builds the starting row by lifting from one and wrapping the other. It's the cast-on most knitters use by default.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from two public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting (first series, 1880s), opening pages on casting on. Internet Archive.
• Mary Thomas's Knitting Book (1938), §Casting on, the long-tail variant. Cross-checked against multiple modern references for the standard slingshot-hand form.

glossaryTerms.definition: The cut end of the yarn coming from the ball, before the slip knot. In a long-tail cast-on the tail provides one of the two strands that build each new stitch.
The left-hand hold used in the long-tail cast-on. The thumb and index finger spread apart with one strand over each; the bottom three fingers grip both strands against the palm.

content.text: A cast-on is the starting row of a knitted piece, the loops the first true row knits into. The long-tail cast-on is the standard option: stretchy enough for most garments, decorative on the edge, and faster once it clicks than the simpler knitted-on alternative.

What makes it "long-tail" is the setup: the yarn is measured to leave a long tail before the slip knot, and the cast-on uses both the tail and the working yarn at the same time. Each new stitch lifts from one strand and wraps the other. The tail gets used up as the cast-on progresses, so the count matters from the start.
What you need
A pair of 4 mm or 5 mm knitting needles and a ball of DK or aran yarn. Practise with about 20 stitches the first few times. Once the rhythm settles, longer cast-ons are no harder, just slower.
Measuring the tail

The standard rule: allow about 2.5 cm of tail per stitch you'll cast on. For 20 stitches that's about 50 cm of tail. For a 200-stitch shawl edge that's 5 m. Better to leave too much than too little: a long-tail cast-on that runs out of tail mid-row has to be undone and started fresh.

Pull out the tail length, then make a slip knot at that point and slip it onto the needle. The tail hangs off the needle on one side; the working yarn (still attached to the ball) hangs off the other.
The slingshot hand
Hold the needle in the right hand with the slip knot on it. The slip knot counts as the first stitch. Both strands hang down from the needle.
Catch the two strands with your left hand and arrange them into the
slingshot hand
The
tail
runs over the thumb.
The working yarn runs over the index finger.
The bottom three fingers close over both strands to grip them against the palm. Open the thumb and index finger so the two strands form a V.
The needle now sits centrally above the V with the slip knot anchored. The strands run down from the needle to the hand below.
Casting on each stitch
For each new stitch after the slip knot:
Move the needle tip down through the loop the tail makes around the thumb, from the front.
Bring the needle tip over the top of the working yarn strand on the index finger, catching it.
Pull the working-yarn strand back down through the thumb loop.
Slip the thumb out of its loop. Tug the tail gently to set the new stitch against the needle.
Re-form the slingshot hand and repeat. Each repetition adds one stitch to the needle. Stop when the needle holds the count you need (counting the slip knot).
Starting the first knit row

Once the cast-on is complete, the needle holding all the stitches transfers to the left hand. The first stitch to be knitted is the last one cast on (the closest to the needle tip). The tail can be woven in later; for now it just hangs at the back of the work. The first row is a standard
knit row
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first long-tail cast-on
attrs.intro: Four things to expect on the first attempt.

items.cause: The tail wasn't measured generously enough at the start, or the cast-on used more tail than the 2.5 cm-per-stitch rule predicted (often the case at thicker yarn weights).
items.fix: Pull the cast-on off the needle, undo back to the slip knot, and start fresh with a longer tail. There's no way to splice tail mid-cast-on.

items.symptom: The cast-on is too tight to knit into
items.cause: The needle was lifted off the strands too sharply, pulling each new stitch firm against the needle.
items.fix: Cast on over both needles held parallel (or over a needle one size larger), then slide the extras out before the first knit row. The cast-on edge sits looser.

items.symptom: The cast-on row is uneven, with some stitches twisted and some loose
items.cause: The slingshot hand kept slipping or re-forming inconsistently between stitches.
items.fix: Pause between stitches and re-form the hand cleanly each time before moving the needle. Speed comes later; consistency comes first.

items.symptom: The first knit row pulls the cast-on into a curl
items.cause: The cast-on tension is much tighter than the first knit row's tension.
items.fix: Knit the first row at the same tension you cast on. If the curl persists, the cast-on was too tight; cast on again over a larger needle.

content.text: Where this goes next

Long-tail is the cast-on most knitters use by default, including for ribbed cuffs and brims. For very stretchy needs (sock cuffs, garments worked in the round from the top down) the tubular cast-on is the next level up. For pure simplicity, the knitted-on cast-on uses one strand only and the needle motion of a knit stitch; it's slower but easier to learn and fine for sample swatches.
